name: Wavelength: Sleep & Jet Lag
subtitle: Measures light, times sleep

screenshots.caption: Is the light you're in helping or hurting your sleep?
screenshots.alt: Daytime reading: 350 melanopic lux, at target, this light is shifting your sleep earlier

screenshots.caption: It tells you exactly when to seek bright light and when to avoid it
screenshots.alt: Outdoor reading: 300 melanopic lux, this light is strengthening your circadian rhythm

screenshots.caption: At night the whole app dims and confirms the room is dark enough
screenshots.alt: Night mode: 3 melanopic lux, well under limit, good, keep it dim

screenshots.caption: Shifting your clock for a new schedule or a new time zone? It guides each step
screenshots.alt: Reading: 350 melanopic lux, this light is shifting your sleep later, avoid caffeine now

description:
Your phone already has the sensor. Point its camera at the room and Wavelength reads the melanopic light reaching your eyes, the blue-rich wavelengths that set your body clock, and tells you in plain language whether it is helping or hurting your sleep right now.

Most circadian apps fall into one of two camps. Light meters hand you a number and leave you to interpret it. Coaching apps build you a sleep plan but never see the light you are actually in. Wavelength is the only one that does both: it measures your real light and coaches your real schedule.

MEASURE

Point your iPhone camera at the light around you. Wavelength estimates melanopic lux, the measure of light that drives your circadian system, and gives you an immediate, color-coded reading with a plain-language verdict: at target, well under limit, or too bright for this hour.

COACH

A number alone changes nothing. Wavelength factors in your current and goal sleep schedule, the time of day, your circadian phase, and the direction you are trying to shift, then tells you whether to seek or avoid light right now and when the next window opens.

DAILY TIMING

Set your current bedtime and your goal bedtime. Wavelength schedules daily reminders:
- When to start dimming before bed
- When bright light will pull your sleep in the right direction
- When light would work against your goal

JET LAG

Crossing time zones is the same engine pointed at a new schedule. Wavelength tells you when to chase light and when to hide from it, so you land closer to local time.

BUILT ON PUBLISHED SCIENCE

Thresholds follow Brown et al. (2022) in PLOS Biology, the expert-consensus recommendations for indoor light:
- Daytime: 250+ melanopic lux for alertness
- Evening: under 10 melanopic lux before bed
- Sleep: under 1 melanopic lux

Phase and amplitude response modeling is based on Hannay et al. (2019) in the Journal of Biological Rhythms.

Better-aligned sleep is also linked in research to steadier mood and daytime focus. Wavelength supports sleep and circadian alignment; it does not diagnose or treat any medical condition.

DESIGNED FOR

- Anyone who wants to sleep better
- Travelers managing jet lag across time zones
- Shift workers holding a rhythm through nights
- Early risers and night owls trying to move their clock

WHAT YOU NEED

An iPhone with a camera. No accessories, no wearable, no extra hardware.

Camera-based measurement is approximate, not a laboratory spectrometer, but well-suited to the relative, in-the-moment comparisons that matter for your body clock.
note:
This is a preview, not the live App Store listing.
Screenshots are the exact 6.7" PNGs from the app build, shown bare as Apple displays them. The third frame is the app's night mode: the whole UI fades to near-black before bed.
